coredata.dc:title: Transfer, transportation, and adsorption of UV-B by Mg-N co doped carbon quantum dots: Response of growth indicators, antioxidant effect and mechanism explanation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu
Huang, Ying
Jiang, Junhong
Chen, Jianbo
Han, Weixing
Liu, Yuxian
Kong, Linjun
Gong, Jian
Su, Minhua
Chen, Diyun
coredata.dc:description: Mg and N co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) were synthesized and applied to alleviate oxygen toxicity by UV-B radiation and enhance antioxidative responses to wheat seedlings. It showed that Mg-N-CQDs pre-treatment attenuated the UV-B stress effects in a dose-dependent manner, as indicated by enhancing the characteristics of seed germination and early seedling growth parameters. Meanwhile, Mg-N-CQDs can be applied in plant nutrient solutions with nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and other fertilizers to promote the growth of seedlings. Furthermore, efficient antioxidant systems, chlorophyll content, and stability of fluorescence intensity were activated by Mg-N-CQDs pre-treatment, which effectively increased the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and peroxidase (POD), and eliminate the contents of malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide, and the production rate of superoxide anion radical in the roots and germs, thereby preventing oxidative damage from UV-B stress. Notably, Mg-N-CQDs pre-treatment significantly increased the expression of related genes to improve the antioxidant capacity of roots and germs, resulting in an increased level of ATPS, CS, and GS. The mechanism study indicated that amino and hydroxyl groups and Mg, N modified CQDs could broaden the light absorption range of CQDs and improve the ability to convert blue light and ultraviolet rays to visible light, which was the main reason why Mg-N-CQDs could relieve wheat seedlings from ultraviolet stress. Therefore, Mg-N-CQDs could serve as a regulator to reduce the damage of UV-B, laying the foundation for their application in environmental protection and agricultural production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mg-N-CQDs
UV-B radiation
Oxidative stress
Amino and hydroxyl groups
Antioxidative mechanism

Absorption and observation of Mg-N-CQDs in wheat seedlings
Effects of Mg-N-CQDs on photosynthetic pigments of wheat seedlings
Changes in flavonoid and antioxidant enzyme activities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Root length (a); plant height (b); root coefficient (c), and fresh weight (d) of wheat in different treatment groups (Mg-N-CQDs: CK 0 mg/ml, C1 0.02 mg/ml, C2 0.04 mg/ml, C3 0.12 mg/ml; UV: UV-B radiation).
Laser confocal images of the wheat root tip and leaf epidermis: a-c were the apical control group; d-f were apical; g-i were root hair; j-l were leaf epidermis.

Chlorophyll content of wheat seedlings in different treatment groups (a); MDA activity of wheat roots and leaves under different treatments (b); The activities of flavonoid (c), SOD (d), POD (e), and CAT (f) of wheat seedlings in different treatments.

(a) TEM of Mg-N-CQDs; (b) HRTEM of Mg-N-CQDs; (c) FT-IR of Mg-N-CQDs; (d) XPS of Mg-N-CQDs; (e) UV–Vis (red line), maximum FL excitation (green line) and emission (blue line) spectra of Mg-N-CQDs [The solution of Mg-N-CQDs in sunlight and ultraviolet light]; (f) Comparison of utilization efficiency of CQDs and Mg-N-CQDs in converting ultraviolet light into visible light [At the excitation wavelength of 300 nm and 360 nm]; XPS single element map of Mg-N-CQDs: (g) C 1 s map; (h) Mg 1 s map; (i) N 1 s map; (j) O 1 s map.
The potential damage caused by UV-B and the antioxidant effect of Mg-N-CQDs.
Analysis results of Mg-N-CQDs by FT-IR.
Analysis results of Mg-N-CQDs by XPS.

head.ce:title: Transfer, transportation, and adsorption of UV-B by Mg-N co doped carbon quantum dots: Response of growth indicators, antioxidant effect and mechanism explanation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Daqing Normal University, Daqing 163712, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Daqing Normal University, Daqing 163712, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mg and N co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) were synthesized and applied to alleviate oxygen toxicity by UV-B radiation and enhance antioxidative responses to wheat seedlings. It showed that Mg-N-CQDs pre-treatment attenuated the UV-B stress effects in a dose-dependent manner, as indicated by enhancing the characteristics of seed germination and early seedling growth parameters. Meanwhile, Mg-N-CQDs can be applied in plant nutrient solutions with nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and other fertilizers to promote the growth of seedlings. Furthermore, efficient antioxidant systems, chlorophyll content, and stability of fluorescence intensity were activated by Mg-N-CQDs pre-treatment, which effectively increased the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and peroxidase (POD), and eliminate the contents of malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide, and the production rate of superoxide anion radical in the roots and germs, thereby preventing oxidative damage from UV-B stress. Notably, Mg-N-CQDs pre-treatment significantly increased the expression of related genes to improve the antioxidant capacity of roots and germs, resulting in an increased level of ATPS, CS, and GS. The mechanism study indicated that amino and hydroxyl groups and Mg, N modified CQDs could broaden the light absorption range of CQDs and improve the ability to convert blue light and ultraviolet rays to visible light, which was the main reason why Mg-N-CQDs could relieve wheat seedlings from ultraviolet stress. Therefore, Mg-N-CQDs could serve as a regulator to reduce the damage of UV-B, laying the foundation for their application in environmental protection and agricultural production.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Mg, N Co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet B (UV-B, 280–350 nm) radiation has the highest energy in the solar spectrum and may damage large molecules (including DNA) that damage some animals and plants and produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) and important cellular processes [1,2]. After UV-B irradiation, the lipid peroxidation of plant seedlings was enhanced, and the activities of antioxidant enzymes involved in the defense system were changed, including catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD) [3]. UV-B radiation leads to a high degree of hydroxylation of flavonoid synthesis, showing strong antioxidant capacity and effectively reducing the concentration of ROS in plant cells [4]. Flavonoids as UV-B filters can absorb UV-B light, have antioxidant and ultraviolet absorption functions, and play a protective role in the process of plants responding to UV-B [5,6]. Therefore, plants can utilize various defense mechanisms to combat the harmful effects of oxidative stress and ROS. ROS was a chemical marker used to predict the degree of abiotic environmental stress. When the balance between ROS and antioxidant defense was disrupted, oxidative stress occurs [7,8]. However, plants have limited antioxidant defense capabilities and self-repair was a time-consuming process. What’s more, due to the fixed lifestyle of plants, they cannot move freely like animals and avoid long-term solar UV-B radiation. Therefore, after being damaged by UV-B radiation, plants need to be repaired manually. However, there was little research on the biological effects of ultraviolet B (UV-B, 280–350 nm) radiation on plant seedlings.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of nanomaterial, have excellent optical properties, and good biocompatibility, making them widely used in UV protection technology [9,10,11,12], optical converter [13,14,15], agricultural production increase [16,17], in particular, CQD, as a plant growth regulator, exhibits excellent UV blocking effects. CQD was a good candidate for UV-resistant materials, and its ability to inhibit UV-mediated biological oxidation was receiving increasing attention, making it an attractive material worth exploring in biological applications. Previous reports have shown that CQDs can be absorbed in plants and transported from roots to leaves [17]. Moreover, CQDs can penetrate plant cell walls and membranes and transport different substances (such as water, ions, and fertilizers) to various organelles of plants [18]. Unfortunately, the reported CQDs usually have amounts of defects, whose fluorescence are limited to blue light with low quantum yields (QYs). When studying the improving quantum yield and biological effects of CQDs on plants, it has been found that doping CQDs with appropriate concentrations of oxides can have a positive impact on plant growth and development, mainly by promoting the growth of plant roots and callus tissues and increasing plant biomass [19]. To further improve the efficiency of quantum yield and phytoremediation, some scholars have proposed strategies to use CQDs to alleviate some abiotic environmental stresses on plants, especially the design and synthesis of CQDs with specific functions, such as doping major elements (e.g., Mg, N, magnetic Fe3O4, and TiO2) with light conduction function, to reduce heavy metal toxicity and UV-B radiation damage of plant seedlings by improving the activity of antioxidant enzymes [20,21,22,23]. However, the current research difficulty was to purposefully synthesize CQDs with a high quantum yield, excellent optical properties, and absorption spectra mainly concentrated in the ultraviolet region. In previous research, XU found that as the doping N content increased, the photoelectric performance of CQDs continued to improve, but there were also limits, possibly due to the saturation of nitrogen atoms in the doping of CQDs [24]. Meanwhile, HUANG proposed two new application strategies for designing CQDs with absorption spectra mainly concentrated in the ultraviolet region. One approach was to construct ultraviolet photovoltaic windows (UPWs), which involved thinning the photoanode to further increase the transparency of the device. In this way, UPWs could generate electricity without affecting indoor lighting and effectively intercept the entry of ultraviolet rays. In another, co-sensitized solar cells were constructed by combining quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSC) and carbon dots (CDs) which had good absorption of visible light, and its PCE was 40.9% higher than that of CDs-QDSCs. The fluorescence could be absorbed by CDs-CQDs, and the light absorption ability of the sensitized photoanode was enhanced; the photoluminescence efficiency of the sensitized photoanode was weakened, indicating that the recombination of electrons and holes was reduced; CDs-CQDs and CQDs form a type I energy level arrangement lowers the potential barrier for electron transfer [25]. However, too much attention has been paid to the application of CQDs to enhance photosynthesis and improve yield, ignoring its efficacy and mechanism in resisting external stress and repair, especially, the research has not been used to protect the protection of the plant from ultraviolet rays. Furthermore, XU discovered that single CQDs were affected by their inherent defects in practical applications, such as light generation electron hole pair easy recombination, limited visible light utilization capacity, and low specific surface area [14]. Specially, the study did not pay attention to CQDs in alleviating plants to be damaged by ultraviolet rays. In addition, the magnesium and nitrogen developed by LIU are mixed with carbon dots (Mg-N-CQDs), which effectively promotes the photosynthetic effect of plants and effectively improves plant growth and yield. However, the mechanism of CQDs and UV-B radiation composite effects on the physiological and biochemical reactions of plants is unclear.

Based on CQDs and light conversion capabilities, as well as the characteristics of easy synthesis and low toxicity, CQDs have shown massive potential in improving a higher quantum yield, excellent optical properties, and absorption spectra mainly concentrated in the ultraviolet region [26]. Herein, the hydrothermal method was used to control the self-doping of CQDs and the doping of heteroatoms (Mg, N, S, P, and other elements), which could improve CQDs' unique optical properties and electronic properties. CQDs' formation and doping mechanism were explored by analyzing their morphological and structural changes. Furthermore, this study aims to use wheat seedlings as the experimental model plant, compare the response indicators of wheat seedlings treated with UV-B radiation and Mg and N co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) alone or in combination, and use quantum dots as modulators to defend against wheat oxidation, to optimize the performance of UV resistance. Besides, this study will enrich the application of Mg-N-CQDs in light sensing, realize the bifunctional coupling of UV light and photosynthesis, and clarify the mechanism of Mg-N-CQDs resisting UV light and promoting plant growth. More importantly, this research will clarify the potential of Mg-N-CQDs in reducing UV-B damage to plants and lay a theoretical foundation for applying Mg-N-CQDs in ecological remediation.

This study synthesized citric acid as a carbon source, urea, and magnesium acetate as doping materials, and Mg-N-CQDs by hydrothermal method. The synthesis process of CQDs and Mg-N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 . Specifically, 3.85 g of citric acid, 2.5 g of urea, and 4.30 g of magnesium acetate were dissolved in 60 ml of ultrapure water, respectively. The dissolved mixture was transferred into the lined autoclave and heated at 180℃ for 14 h. The sample was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged for 30 min (10 000 r/min). Then the supernatant was filtered by a 0.22 µm filter element. The filtrate was collected and frozen at −20 ℃ for 24 h. Finally, the Mg-N-CQDs solid was obtained by freeze-drying. Calculated quantum yield of the as synthesized Mg-N-CQDs (Text S1 and
Figs. S1 - S2 ). All chemical reagents were both analytically pure, produced by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ultrapure water was used as a diluent during the experiments.

This study aims to use wheat seedlings as the experimental model plant, compare the response indicators of wheat seedlings treated with UV-B radiation and Mg-N-CQDs alone or in combination, and use CQDs as modulators to defend against wheat oxidation to optimize the performance of UV resistance.

Wheat seeds were soaked in sterilized water for one hour and then sterilized with 1.5% (V/V) sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) for 10 min. Then, the NaClO on the surface of the seeds was washed with sterilized water, and the seeds in the culture dish were covered with soaked gauze. The seeds were cultured in a 25 ℃ incubator in the dark for 24 h to germinate (subject to the seeds being white). Finally, the germinated seeds were seeded in a Petri dish covered with gauze.

UV-B treatment: The adjustable UV-B lamp holder was used, with the lamp tube placed 1 m above the plant population. The control CK was under natural light, and the treatment T was under UV radiation enhancement of 0.70 W/m2, equivalent to an average increase of 8% in summer UV radiation in Guangzhou. It was irradiated for 9 h from 8:00 to 17:00 daily and repeated thrice. The indicators for wheat seedlings include (1) morphology and growth indicators: root length, plant height, root coefficient, and biomass accumulation (fresh weight). (2) Physiological indicators: expression levels of chlorophyll synthesis, lipid peroxidation, flavonoids, antioxidant enzymes, and antioxidant regulatory genes.

Two groups of wheat were cultured with different concentrations of Mg-N-CQDs (CK 0 mg/ml, C1 0.02 mg/ml, C2 0.05 mg/ml, and C3 0.12 mg/ml) every day, and one group was added with UV-B radiation (10.08 kJ/(m2 d)) at the same time, Optical instrument factory) were treated with compound treatment (UV, U + C1, U + C2, U + C3) and put into the intelligent light incubator with a temperature of 22 ℃ and the Photoperiod of 9 h for five days, and Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution exposed to sunlight and ultraviolet as shown in Fig. S5
(in Supplementary Material
s).

The toxicity of Mg-N-CQDs to HeLa cells was detected by Cell Counting Kitcck-8 (CCK-8). HeLa cell suspension was placed in 96 healthy cell culture plates, incubated in 5% CO2 incubator at 37 ℃ for 24 h, and then cultured with Mg-N-CQDs solution of different concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, and 1.0 mg/ml) in complete culture medium (90% DMEM + 10% fetal serum) for 12 h (the group with 0 mg/ml was used as control group). Importantly, fertilizers (e.g., N, P, and K.) were applied to investigate the effects of Mg-N-CQDs-containing culture medium on the growth characteristics of plant seedlings. Nitrogen (urea, N mass fraction was 46%), phosphorus (superphosphate, P2O5 mass fraction was 14%), and potassium (potassium sulfate, K mass fraction was 60%) were used as experimental fertilizers, respectively, and non-fertilization treatment was used as a control group (CK); Three fertilizer combinations were designed: T1 (N 0.2 mg/ml, P 0.20 mg/ml, LK 0.20 mg/ml and Mg-N-CQDs 0.8 mg/ml), T2 (N 0.50 mg/ml, P 0.50 mg/ml, K 0.50 mg/ml and Mg-N-CQDs 0.8 mg/ml), and T3 (N 0.80 mg/ml, P 0.80 mg/ml, K 0.80 mg/ml and Mg-N-CQDs 0.8 mg/ml). After three repetitions of each treatment for five days, the effects of N, P, K, and Mg-N-CQDs co-fertilization on wheat growth characteristics were analyzed using the root length, plant height, root number, and fresh weight of wheat seedlings as evaluation indicators. A microplate reader measured the absorbance of the hole.
Cell viability was calculated by the equation cell viability = A/A0 × 100%.
where A was for absorbance of the experimental group; A0 was for absorbance of the control group. Each measurement was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of biomass and length of wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: After five days, the wheat seedlings were removed from the hydroponic system. Use filter paper to absorb the water from the root. The fresh weight of single seedlings was measured by sartorius electronic balance (BSA224S). The root length and plant height were measured with a steel ruler and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and observation of Mg-N-CQDs in wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: The 3-day-old wheat seedlings in 0 and 0.02 mg/ml of Mg-N-CQDs treatment groups were used to observe the absorption and distribution of Mg-N-CQDs. The root tip and leaf epidermis were observed by Olympus laser confocal microscope. FV-1000 software was used to obtain images. The observation of root and leaf cells was repeated at least three times. Mg-N-CQDs were excited at 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of chlorophyll content in wheat seedling leaves
ce:section.ce:para:
The method of Lichtenthaler was modified to measure chlorophyll content [27]. 0.1 g of leaves were placed in the test tube, and 10 ml of 95% ethanol was added to the test tube. The leaves were soaked in the dark at 25 ℃ for one day until all the leaves were chlorotic. Took an appropriate amount of the substance to be tested, immersed in 95% ethanol solution, ground thoroughly, used a centrifuge to separate for 3 min, extracted the supernatant, and diluted to constant volume. The absorbance values at 663 nm, 645 nm, 470 nm and UV–Visible spectra were observed on the UV spectrophotometer (UV–VIS SPECTROPHOTOMETER, UV-1900i, SHIMADZU), and the chlorophyll content was calculated according to the absorbance values. The calculation formula is as follows:
Chl a = 12.7A663 − 2.69A645
Chl b = 22.9A645 − 4.68A663
Total Chlorophyll = Chl a + Chl b = 20.2A645 + 8.02A663

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MDA, flavonoids, and antioxidant enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: The roots and leaves of each treatment group were cut and mixed. Then 0.1 g of leaves and roots were weighed to determine the plant tissue's physiological and biochemical indexes. Lipid peroxidation was an indicator of abiotic stress and can be measured indirectly by malondialdehyde (MDA) formation. The activities of MDA, flavonoids, SOD (EC 1.15.1.1), POD (EC 1.11.1.7), and CAT (EC 1.11.1.6) were detected by the ELISA kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific - CN).

In order to determine the expression level of antioxidant regulatory genes in wheat seedlings, total RNA was extracted from 5-day-old seedlings by the TRIzol method and then subjected to a reverse transcription kit to obtain the cDNA strand. Real-time PCR detected the changes in antioxidant-related genes in wheat seedlings. The relative expression of genes was calculated by comparing 2−ΔΔCt. The internal reference gene is actin. All primer sequences are shown in Table S1 .

The morphology of Mg-N-CQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), respectively. Information on element composition, content, chemical state, molecular structure, and chemical bond of Mg-N-CQDs was tested by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The molecular structure and chemical composition of Mg-N-CQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra obtained by Nicolet 380 infrared spectrometer (Varian, USA).
All experiments were repeated three times. Origin 2018 was used for data analysis and mapping, and Endnote X9 was used to insert references.

The chemical reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade (purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), and all samples were in triplicate to ensure precision and accuracy. Besides, all dilutions were performed with MilliQ + deionized water. In this study, the percentage error formula was used to calculate the deviation degree between the measurement results and the standard value, which could represent the error size in the measurement process. Its formula (1) is:
(1)
% e r r o r =
Exact
v a l u e - A p p r o x i m a t e
v a l u e
Exact
v a l u e
× 100
Since the number of determinations was often more than one, the analytical results were usually represented by the arithmetic mean of several parallel determinations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological indicators of wheat on UV-B response to sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: Although the number of roots was not directly affected by UV-B, the inhibition of photosynthesis by UV-B led to the inhibition of the growth of aboveground roots and the corresponding reduction of organic nutrients transported to the roots, so the roots were indirectly affected by UV-B. Table S2 shows a marked shortening of the root system after UV-B irradiation. Meanwhile, plant height was generally considered an indicator of plant sensitivity to UV-B. It significantly impacted wheat's lodging resistance and yield composition. Table 1 showed that plant height was more sensitive to UV-B response, which may be related to the change of endogenous hormone content and plant distribution by UV-B radiation (see Table 1).

In contrast, flavonoids could be used to indicate plant adaptation to UV-B. However, the tolerance of plant flavonoids to adapt to ultraviolet radiation was limited, which required human intervention and repair. Further research was needed on the resistance limit of wheat to ultraviolet radiation under intense UV-A radiation and the impact of CQDs on wheat growth indicators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Mg-N-CQDs on wheat growth
ce:section.ce:para:
Root length, plant height, root coefficient, and biomass accumulation (measured by fresh weight) were initially analyzed to evaluate the damage to seedlings by UV-B radiation and the effect of Mg-N-CQDs on seedling restoration. In this study, UV-B showed damage to wheat and caused severe damage to its growth indicators. As shown in Fig. 2
(a-d), the root length, plant height, and fresh weight of wheat exposed to UV-B radiation decreased significantly (as high as 39.8%, 57.4%, and 35.3%, respectively), indicating that solid UV-B radiation seriously inhibits the growth of wheat and affect various physiological indexes, which indicated that UV-B radiation was negatively correlated with wheat growth traits. However, the growth of wheat could be promoted by Mg-N-CQDs. Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2 d showed that wheat's root length and fresh weight reach significant levels after Mg-N-CQDs cultivation. However, the plant height has no significant difference compared with the control group (Fig. 2 b), consistent with the results of previous studies that CDs could promote the growth of mung beans [28]. When wheat seedlings were treated with UV-B and Mg-N-CQDs, the root length, plant height, and fresh weight of Mg-N-CQDs were significantly higher than those of UV-B alone, which indicated that Mg-N-CQDs could alleviate the inhibition of UV-B on plant growth in the presence of UV-B. Due to the degradation of CQDs after entering the plant body and the production of plant hormone analogs, thus promoting the growth of plants. It may also be that nanocarbon enters root cells and changes the metabolic rate, leading to increased auxin content in the root. Moreover, the increase in root activity was conducive to absorbing more water and promoting plant growth rapidly, indicating that Mg-N-CQDs can promote plant growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and observation of Mg-N-CQDs in wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para:
The absorption and distribution of Mg-N-CQDs in the roots and leaves of wheat were observed by confocal laser scanning. As shown in Fig. 3
(a-l), the fluorescence intensity in root tips of wheat was more substantial than that in leaves, which was mainly distributed in the cell membrane and cytoplasm of root tip cells (Fig. 3 (d-f)). However, no fluorescence was detected in the control group without Mg-N-CQDs (Fig. 3 (a-c)). Meanwhile, fluorescence was also observed in root hairs (Fig. 3 (g-i)), indicating that Roots had absorbed Mg-N-CQDs. Besides, fluorescence was also detected in the leaf epidermis (Fig. 3 (j-l)), mainly located in the leaf epidermis and vascular bundles, indicating that Mg-N-CQDs have been transported to the leaves, which was consistent with the report that fluorescence was observed in the roots and leaves of Arabidopsis thaliana by laser confocal microscopy [29]. Some studies have also proved that CQDs could penetrate through the cell wall of plants and enter into cells, which could be absorbed and enriched in the cell membrane and cytoplasm, causing weak fluorescence in the nucleus [30]. There were about 10 nm stomata on the cell wall of plant cells, which could regulate the transport of small molecules. Therefore, the small-sized of Mg-N-CQDs (less than 5 nm) could enter cells through the pore size of the cell wall, demonstrating that Mg-N-CQDs penetrate plants with an uptake sequence from root to leaf.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Mg-N-CQDs on photosynthetic pigments of wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para:
Chlorophyll is an essential physiological parameter in plant growth and development, and different stress conditions could cause changes in photosynthetic pigment content [31]. In Fig. 4
a, the total chlorophyll content of the plants treated with Mg-N-CQDs was significantly higher than that of the control group. However, the total chlorophyll content decreased significantly after UV-B irradiation. After Mg-N-CQDs and UV-B treatment, the total chlorophyll content increased but was lower than that of the control group. It was found that CDs could also increase the content of chlorophyll. Both single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes significantly increased the expression level of genes related to chloroplast development [32 33], which was consistent with the fact that Mg-N-cd can alleviate the decrease in chlorophyll content caused by UV-B radiation to a certain extent.

The presence of toxic ROS could explain the accumulation of Malondialdehyde (MDA) levels [34]. Tissues in seedlings may be damaged as ROS production exceeds the ability of plant antioxidant mechanisms to scavenge ROS and protect plant cells [35]. In Fig. 4 b, MDA activity in leaves increased after C1, C2, and C3 treatment, indicating that the cells were subjected to oxidative stress but could still carry out normal biosynthesis. However, when exposed to UV-B radiation, the MDA activity in leaves decreased significantly, indicating that the cells were seriously damaged and could not be biosynthesized normally, resulting in malondialdehyde could not continue to be produced or even degraded, so the activity decreased. Severe cell damage was caused by direct exposure of leaves to UV-B radiation. However, after UV-B and Mg-N-CQDs treatment, MDA activity in U + C1 and U + C2 groups increased by about 18% compared with UV-B treatment alone, indicating that the damage degree of leaf cells was alleviated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in flavonoid and antioxidant enzyme activities
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of UV-B radiation and Mg-N-CQDs particles on cellular oxidative stress by generating ROS were evaluated for the potential damage of UV-B and Mg-N-CQDs at the cellular level in wheat. Meanwhile, this study also described a variety of cell antioxidant and detoxification methods, including the expression of oxidative stress defense genes. Flavonoid was an antioxidant used as a defense against ROS damage in plants. It was also a secondary metabolite produced by the plant stress response. Fig. 4 c represented the flavonoid activity of the leaf and root, which was improved under Mg-N-CQDs treatment. After UV-B radiation, the activity of flavonoids in roots decreased significantly, indicating that higher intensity of UV-B radiation could inhibit the synthesis of flavonoids. However, the flavonoid activity of UV-B combined with Mg-N-CQDs was significantly higher than that of UV-B alone, indicating that Mg-N-CQDs activated the production of flavonoids and improved antioxidant activity.

Meanwhile, plant cells have antioxidant defense systems composed of enzymes such as SOD, POD, and CAT. The enzymatic effect of SOD can produce H2O2 and O2, which POD and CAT could remove. It can be transformed into water and oxygen, relieving the poisoning of H2O2 [36]. Furthermore, plants could use a variety of defense mechanisms to resist the harmful effects of oxidative stress and ROS. When the balance between ROS and antioxidant defense is destroyed, oxidative stress occurs [37]. Besides, it has been reported that many nanomaterials, such as ZnO nanoparticles, Fe2O3 nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, and biochar, have been proven to be able to reduce the damage of ROS to plants by inducing higher antioxidant enzyme activities [38]. Fig. 4 d showed that the SOD activity in wheat leaves and roots treated with UV-B decreased by about 12% compared with the control group, consistent with the results reported in the literature that high-intensity UV-B radiation can reduce the SOD activity of wheat seedlings. When the function of SOD in scavenging excess ROS was weakened, the activity of SOD was also inhibited by the combined treatment of UV-B and Mg-N-CQDs. Meantime, POD activity in the roots and leaves of wheat seedlings increased with the increase of Mg-N-CQDs concentration (Fig. 4 e). After UV-B treatment, the POD activity in leaves and roots was significantly decreased by 22.3% and 12.1% In compared In with the control group. However, after UV-B was combined with C1 and C2 groups, the POD activity in leaves was significantly increased, and the POD activity in roots was significantly increased in all groups. It has been proved that N-CDs can significantly improve the activity of PODs to improve the antioxidant capacity of plants, indicating that Mg-N-CQDs can enhance the antioxidant effect of plants and alleviate the damage caused by UV-B [29]. Compared with the control group, the CAT activity of all root treatment groups was significantly increased. After UV-B and Mg-N-CQDs combined treatment, the CAT activity was significantly higher than that of UV-B alone treatment (Fig. 4 f), indicating that Mg-N-CQDs activated CAT activity. After UV-B treatment, the CAT activity of leaves decreased, which proved that with the increase of UV-B radiation intensity, the CAT activity of wheat seedlings also decreased. The CAT activity increased by 17.3% and 20.8% after UV-B was combined with C1 and C2 groups, respectively, indicating that Mg-N-CQDs alleviated the damage of UV-B to plants. The CAT activity of leaves under UV-B treatment was similar to that of the control. However, the combination of C1, C2, and UV-B activated part of the antioxidant function of wheat leaves. Therefore, UV-B radiation may cause excessive production of ROS, which will be eliminated by antioxidant enzymes. However, if reactive oxygen species are not removed in time, it will cause certain damage to the plant cell membrane and destroy the plant antioxidant defense system. In conclusion, Mg-N-CQDs can partially alleviate the antioxidant reaction induced by UV-B. These enzymatic antioxidants provide the necessary protective mechanism, which can reduce the oxidative damage of plants, indicating the potential value of Mg-N-CQDs in phytoremediation.

Glutathione is a significant antioxidant in cells. Therefore, several vital genes (ATPS, CS, GS) in its synthesis process were screened and used to study the response of UV-B to plants, and the relative expression of these three genes was analyzed by real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR. Fig. S7
a showed that after UV-B radiation, the relative expression level of ATPS was 31.6% lower than that of the control group, resulting in the decrease of adenosine 5′- phosphosulfate (APS) content. However, the main pathway was that ATPS could be deoxidized to sulfite and then used to synthesize cysteine, the precursor of glutathione biosynthesis ( Fig. S6 ) [39]. Once the expression of ATPS was inhibited, the synthesis of glutathione would be reduced.

Meanwhile, the expression levels of CS and GS were significantly higher than those in the control group after UV-B radiation ( Figs. S7
(b-c)). However, after combining Mg-N-CQDs and UV-B combined treatment, the expression level of ATPS in all treatment groups was higher than in the UV-B-only treatment group, indicating that Mg-N-CQDs could indirectly reduce the inhibitory effect of UV-B on glutathione synthesis. In this study, wheat seedlings increased the expression of related genes except for ATPS to synthesize glutathione to resist oxidative stress efficiently. The results showed that Mg-N-CQDs could indirectly improve the antioxidant capacity at the molecular level, which was consistent with the previous results of plant physiology. More importantly, this study explained the potential of Mg-N-CQDs to protect plants from UV-B damage at the molecular level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High quantum yield and stability of fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
A detailed calculation of the Quantum yield of the as synthesized Mg-N-CQD in Text S1 showed that the QY of Mg-N-CQDs was determined to be 58.8%, which was higher than the currently reported CQDs, N-CQDs, and Mg-CQDs, indicating a high QY of Mg-N-CQD ( Figs. S1 - S2 ). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs under different conditions was examined to investigate further the fluorescence stability of Mg-N-CQDs and subsequent biological applications. Figs. S6
(d-e) showed the fluorescence stability of Mg-N-CQDs at 320 nm under different pH, ionic strength, and time, respectively. Multiple pH gradients (4–9) were set to detect the fluorescence (FL) intensity of Mg-N-CQDs at different pH values. Fig. S7
d showed that the FL intensity of Mg-N-CQDs did not fluctuate greatly when the pH was 4.0–7.0, so the neutral environment with a pH value of 7.0 was selected for subsequent biological experiments. Meantime, to test the stability of FL of Mg-N-CQDs at high salinity, they were dissolved in different concentrations of NaCl solution. FL intensity was recorded ( Fig. S7
e). The results showed that the FL intensity of Mg-N-CQDs remained unchanged when NaCl in concentration was from 0.2 to 1.0 M, indicated that the FL intensity of Mg-N-CQDs was not affected by salt concentration, and it had good stability. Importantly, the effect of different times (1, 10, 20, and 30 h) on FL intensity in PBS solution with pH 7.0 was shown in Fig. S7
f, indicating that time did not affect the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs. More importantly, the interference experience of Mg-N-CQDs indicated that the reproducibility of different electrodes of Mg-N-CQDs/Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was better, and the Mg-N-CQDs/GCE electrode has excellent specificity, selectivity, and reproducibility (Text S3 and Figs. S9 - S10 ). Therefore, the effect of Mg-N-CQDs on wheat seedlings has a stable performance of fluorescence intensity.

The size and morphology of Mg-N-CQDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 5
a, the particle diameters of Mg-N-CQDs were less than 5.0 nm and had good dispersibility. HRTEM images (Fig. 5 b) clearly show that the lattice spacing of Mg-N-CQDs was 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [40]. FTIR could preliminarily analyze the surface functional groups of Mg-N-CQDs, and the infrared spectrum could be obtained. Fig. 5 c showed wide absorption bands at 3418 cm−1 and 3220 cm−1, caused by the vibration of the O–H and N–H bonds. The peak detected at 1560 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of the C = O bond. The peak at 1077 cm−1 indicated the tensile vibration of C-O-C (Table 1). FTIR results showed abundant chromogenic groups (hydroxyl and amino) on the surface of Mg-N-CQDs, consistent with the excellent ultraviolet protection performance of Mg-N-CQDs.

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used further to study the surface structure and elements of Mg-N-CQDs, consistent with the single-element analysis results (Fig. 5 d). Four peaks are shown in Fig. 5 (g-j), representing the surface presence of C1s (285.35 eV), N1s (400.81 eV), O1s (529.81 eV), and Mg1s (1303.48 eV) on Mg-N-CQDs, respectively (Table 2 ), which verified the designability of the CQDs structure. For example, by introducing chromophores such as amino and hydroxyl groups (Fig. 5 (i-j)) into CQDs, the visible light absorption ability of CQDs can be enhanced, empowering CQDs with efficient photocatalytic ability under low energy visible light and even sunlight irradiation. Meanwhile, Mg-N-CQDs achieve the conversion of ultraviolet light into visible light, thereby reducing ultraviolet radiation. The introduction of amino (- NH2) and hydroxyl (- OH) groups on CQDs to improve their visible light catalytic performance has been widely confirmed [12,11]. Importantly, to test the performance of Mg-N-CQDs in converting ultraviolet light into visible light, the optical properties of Mg-N-CQDs were measured by fluorescence spectrometer. Fig. 5 e shows UV–Vis spectrum of Mg-N-CQDs in the near ultraviolet region, with a maximum absorption wavelength of 225 nm, which shifts towards the red compared to N-CQDs ( Fig.S8 ). When the excitation wavelength was 320 nm, the maximum emission peak of Mg-N-CQDs was 410 nm. The illustration in Fig. 5 e showed the dispersion of Mg-N-CQDs in aqueous solution under sunlight and ultraviolet light, indicating the ability of Mg-N-CQDs to convert ultraviolet light into visible light and improve their absorption performance for ultraviolet light.

More importantly, the fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum of Mg-N-CQDs at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm indicated that Mg-N-CQDs had excellent fluorescence properties. The possible reason was that the doped Mg reacted with the oxygen-containing moieties in Mg-N-CQDs, decreasing the energy levels and increasing the vibration relaxation of the energy levels [41].

Besides, CQDs were widely studied due to their visible solid light response, excellent chemical and thermal stability, and low cost. However, single CQDs were also affected by their inherent defects in practical applications, such as easy recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, limited visible light utilization ability, and low specific surface area. As shown in Fig. 5 f, compared with a single CQDs, the coupling of CQDs with other semiconductors (Mg/N) to synthesize heterostructures could achieve effective separation of electron-hole pairs and broaden the light response range, demonstrating excellent photocatalytic performance through synergistic effects. Modifying CQDs doped with N and Mg (Fig. 5 (h-i)) enhanced their light absorption ability in the visible light region. Significantly, Mg-N-CQDs as photosensitizers could effectively improve the utilization efficiency of ultraviolet light conversion into visible light (Fig. 5 f) [42].

In this study, a Mg, N co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) for seedlings with dispersive, high quantum yield, excellent optical properties, highly absorption spectra mainly concentrated in the ultraviolet region, and transfer of ultraviolet light was synthesized, which was favorable for uptake by plant cells and alleviated the oxidative damage of UV-B and enhanced antioxidative responses to wheat seedlings for its small particle size and unique physicochemical properties. The root inhibition, biomass reduction, and oxidative damage of wheat seedlings under UV-B exposure can be reduced by Mg-N-CQDs. Applying Mg-N-CQDs to wheat after UV-B treatment can activate defense enzymes, enhance antioxidants, reduce plant damage, and promote increased chlorophyll content. Furthermore, glutathione-related genes were also analyzed, and the results showed that Mg-N-CQDs could improve the antioxidant capacity of plants by regulating gene expression and played a positive role in improving the UV-B radiation damage of wheat. Importantly, Mg-N-CQDs were a safe and effective dose without biotoxicity to wheat seedlings, which can be applied in plant nutrient solutions with nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. Besides, IR and XPS, as well as experimental testing, indicated that chromophore functions such as amino and hydroxyl groups and Mg, N modified CQDs could broaden the light absorption range of CQDs and improve the ability to convert blue light and ultraviolet rays to visible light, which was the main reason why Mg-N-CQDs could relieve wheat seedlings from ultraviolet stress. Therefore, Mg-N-CQDs can serve as a regulator to reduce the damage of UV-B to plants, laying the foundation for their application in environmental protection and agricultural production. In conclusion, this work provided a new way to improve the tolerance of wheat seedlings to UV-B radiation, but further research is needed to explain the potential molecular mechanism of the tolerance of Mg-N-CQDs to other plants under UV-B stress.

Yu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ying Huang: Funding acquisition, Software, Visualization, Project administration, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Junhong Jiang: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Investigation. Jianbo Chen: Methodology, Software, Data curation. Weixing Han: Methodology, Software. Yuxian Liu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Linjun Kong: Validation, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jian Gong: Writing - review & editing. Minhua Su: Writing - review & editing. Diyun Chen: Funding acquisition, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22276043; 42230719; 22306036; 52200188), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFC1904101; 2019YFC1904102), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong province (2023A1515011808), Postdoctoral Science Foundation Starting of Guangzhou City (62216272), 2021 Guangzhou Science and Technology Innovation and Development Special Fund (the first batch) basic and applied basic research project (202102020287).
The authors thank Qingyu Zhang and Yaping Li from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the SEM and TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:para:
